questions.q: In which present-day Indian state is Daulatabad Fort located?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad Fort stands near Aurangabad (Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar) in the state of Maharashtra.

questions.q: Daulatabad Fort is built on a hill historically known by what earlier name?
questions.explanation: The fort was originally called Devagiri, meaning 'Hill of the Gods', before being renamed Daulatabad.

questions.q: Daulatabad Fort sits closest to which major city of Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: The fort lies about 15 km from Aurangabad, now officially renamed Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar.

questions.q: What distinctive natural shape characterizes the central hill of Daulatabad Fort?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad is famous for its steep, conical hill, which made the citadel exceptionally hard to storm.

questions.q: Which type of monument is Daulatabad best described as?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad is a formidable hill fort, long regarded as one of the most powerful fortresses in the Deccan.

questions.q: The name 'Daulatabad' broadly translates to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad means 'Abode of Wealth' or 'City of Fortune', a name given when it was made an imperial capital.

questions.q: Which Delhi Sultanate ruler famously made Daulatabad his capital?
questions.explanation: Sultan Muhammad bin Tughlaq shifted his capital from Delhi to Daulatabad in the 14th century.

questions.q: Daulatabad Fort is best known as a defensive structure rather than a place of what use?
questions.explanation: The fort's reputation rests on its military strength, with concentric walls and natural defences.

questions.q: Within the Daulatabad complex stands a tall victory tower called the Chand Minar. What is it?
questions.explanation: The Chand Minar is a soaring minaret within the fort, a prominent landmark of the complex.

questions.q: Daulatabad Fort lies in which broad geographical region of India?
questions.explanation: The fort is situated on the Deccan Plateau, a strategic crossroads of medieval Indian power.

questions.q: Devagiri rose to prominence as the capital of which Deccan dynasty before the Sultanate era?
questions.explanation: Devagiri was the capital of the Yadava (Seuna) dynasty before it fell to the Delhi Sultanate.

questions.q: Muhammad bin Tughlaq's transfer of his capital to Daulatabad took place in roughly which decade?
questions.explanation: The capital shift occurred around 1327, in the early years of Muhammad bin Tughlaq's reign.

questions.q: Why is Muhammad bin Tughlaq's capital shift to Daulatabad remembered as a failure?
options: The fort collapsed in an earthquake
The move caused great hardship and the capital was soon shifted back to Delhi
questions.explanation: The forced migration from Delhi caused enormous suffering, and within a few years the capital reverted to Delhi.

questions.q: Which Yadava ruler was reigning at Devagiri when Alauddin Khalji first raided it in 1296?
questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji's celebrated 1296 raid struck Devagiri during the reign of the Yadava king Ramachandra.

questions.q: The Chand Minar at Daulatabad is generally attributed to which Deccan sultanate?
questions.explanation: The Chand Minar is traditionally credited to the Bahmani ruler Alauddin Bahman Shah's successors in the 15th century.

questions.q: A famous defensive feature of Daulatabad is a dark, winding passage cut into the rock. What is it designed to do?
options: Confuse and trap attackers approaching the citadel
questions.explanation: The labyrinthine, pitch-dark passage was built to disorient and trap enemies trying to reach the upper fort.

questions.q: Around the base of the conical citadel, attackers also had to cross what additional obstacle?
questions.explanation: A deep moat carved around the hill formed part of the fort's layered defences.

questions.q: Daulatabad Fort is notable for having multiple lines of defence arranged in what pattern?
options: Walls only on the river side
questions.explanation: The fort is protected by several concentric circuits of walls, gates, and bastions surrounding the central hill.

questions.q: Which empire controlled Daulatabad before it eventually came under Mughal authority in the Deccan?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad served as a stronghold of the Ahmadnagar (Nizam Shahi) Sultanate before falling to the Mughals.

questions.q: Daulatabad's strategic value in the medieval Deccan came mainly from its control over what?
options: Important inland routes through the Deccan
questions.explanation: Sitting on the Deccan Plateau, the fort commanded key overland routes, making it a coveted prize for rival powers.

questions.q: What was a primary building material used for the formidable walls and bastions of Daulatabad Fort?
questions.explanation: The fort's massive walls and bastions were built largely of locally quarried stone, exploiting the rocky hill.

questions.q: The dynasty associated with the original Devagiri capital is also called by which alternative name?
questions.explanation: The Yadavas of Devagiri are also known as the Seuna dynasty.

questions.q: Alauddin Khalji's 1296 raid on Devagiri was launched while he held what position?
options: He was already Sultan of Delhi
He was governor of Kara, before becoming Sultan
questions.explanation: Alauddin raided Devagiri as the ambitious governor of Kara, using its wealth to seize the Delhi throne soon after.

questions.q: The lower fortified town spreading out from the citadel at Daulatabad is sometimes called by what name?
questions.explanation: The outermost fortified enclosure of the town is known as the Ambarkot, encircling the wider settlement.

questions.q: Within the Daulatabad complex stands a Sultanate-era congregational mosque built by reusing Hindu and Jain temple pillars, later converted into a Bharat Mata temple. By what name is it known?
questions.explanation: The fort's 14th-century Jama Masjid, raised with pillars taken from earlier temples, was converted into a Bharat Mata temple after 1947; the Quwwat-ul-Islam, Moti Masjid and Sidi Saiyyed mosques are elsewhere in India.

questions.q: A large cannon mounted at Daulatabad, sometimes bearing inscriptions, reflects the fort's use of artillery during which broad period?
options: The medieval to early-modern Deccan sultanate/Mughal period
questions.explanation: Heavy cannon were installed on the bastions during the sultanate and Mughal era, when gunpowder artillery dominated Deccan warfare.

questions.q: After the Mughal decline, Daulatabad and the surrounding region eventually fell under the control of which power?
options: The Nizams of Hyderabad (Asaf Jahi dynasty)
questions.explanation: The fort passed to the Nizams of Hyderabad (Asaf Jahi dynasty), who held the Deccan region until India's independence era.

questions.q: Daulatabad's defensive design is admired because the steep citadel could only be reached by what?
options: A single narrow, easily defended approach
An open grassy slope on all sides
questions.explanation: The summit was accessible only through a narrow, heavily fortified single approach, including the famous dark tunnel.

questions.q: Devagiri's strategic importance grew because it lay near the boundary between the north and which cultural-political sphere?
questions.explanation: Devagiri/Daulatabad sat at the gateway between northern India and the Deccan, making it pivotal for any power expanding southward.

questions.q: Today, Daulatabad Fort is officially protected and maintained primarily by which body?
questions.explanation: Daulatabad Fort is a protected monument under the care of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI).
